variables.content: Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

The proposition: For Fermi liquid parameters $F_l$ (or $F_l/m^{*}$) of the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) computed in square periodic simulation cells containing $N$ electrons, the leading systematic finite-size correction arising from long-range correlation effects scales asymptotically as $N^{\gamma}$ with exponent $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., a correction term proportional to $N^{-1/4}$), so that finite-$N$ results can be extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting to the form $F_l(N)=c+aN^{-1/4}$; this scaling is the same as that argued in the theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation finite-size effects (Holzmann et al.).

For the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG), the leading finite-size error in Fermi-liquid-derived quantities such as the angular-momentum Fermi liquid parameters F_l or the Fourier-projected quantities F_l/m^{*} behaves asymptotically as a power law in the finite-system particle number N with exponent -1/4; equivalently,
      \Delta F_l(N) \equiv F_l(N)-F_l(\infty) \propto N^{-1/4},
      so that fitting computed F_l(N) values to the form c + a N^{-1/4} yields an estimate of the thermodynamic-limit intercept c = F_l(\infty). This statement adopts the theoretical asymptotic exponent \gamma = -1/4 predicted for long-range-correlation-dominated finite-size errors.
      Refs. 13,14

The extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses exhibit distinct density dependences depending on spin polarization: for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10 the extrapolated effective mass remains close to unity (\\(m^{*}\\approx1\\)), indicating no significant interaction-induced mass enhancement at these densities for the clean continuum 2D HEG model considered; by contrast, for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG the extrapolated effective mass decreases as the density is lowered, for example taking the values \\(0.85(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=1\\), \\(0.74(1)\\) at \\(r_{s}=5\\), and \\(0.70(3)\\) at \\(r_{s}=10\\), a trend that is qualitatively consistent with experimental measurements on spin-polarized two-dimensional systems reported in the literature (e.g., Padmanabhan et al.).

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

For a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) simulated in a periodically repeated finite simulation cell containing N electrons, let E_N denote the computed energy per electron of that N-electron finite system and let E_∞ denote the energy per electron in the thermodynamic limit (infinite system size). The proposition is: the dominant many-body finite-size bias in the energy per electron for the fluid phase falls off for large N as a single leading asymptotic term proportional to N^{-5/4}, i.e., E_N = E_∞ - c N^{-5/4} + o(N^{-5/4}) for some constant c, and furthermore this asymptotic N^{-5/4} dependence provides a quantitatively valid description for the finite system sizes employed in the reported calculations (specifically for system sizes up to about N = 162), so that fitting finite-N results to that leading form yields a meaningful extrapolation to E_∞.
      N.D. Drummond et al., Phys. Rev. B 78, 125106 (2008)

Let \gamma(D) denote the power-law exponent entering the large-\sigma asymptotic \mathcal{R}(\boldsymbol{r})\sim C(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}},u)\,\sigma^{-\gamma}\exp(-\sigma/\xi); explicit calculations in the chessboard tight-binding model at zero temperature with a completely filled lower band yield \gamma(1)=1/2 for D=1 and \gamma(2)=1 for D=2, and the paper infers the general rule \gamma(D)=D/2 for these computed cases; the caveat is that this inference is based on the model's spectral structure (two symmetric bands and the specific Brillouin-zone integral representation used) and on the computed directions, so the claimed universality \gamma=D/2 outside the explicitly computed settings requires the assumption that no subtle direction-dependent corrections or boundary-condition effects alter the exponent.

For two-dimensional periodic systems of area $P$ containing $N$ electrons, if the small-$k$ translationally averaged static structure factor has the RPA-motivated form $S(k)=\gamma k^{3/2}+O(k^2)$ with coefficient $\gamma>0$, then the leading finite-size correction to the Ewald exchange–correlation (XC) energy per particle scales with particle number as $O(N^{-1/4})$ and has the explicit large-$N$ form
      $$
      \Delta V_{\mathrm{Ew}}=\frac{C_{2\mathrm{D}}}{2\pi r_s^3}\left(\frac{2}{\pi N}\right)^{1/4}+O(N^{-1/2}),
      $$
      where $r_s$ is defined in 2D by $P/N=\pi r_s^2$ or as otherwise appropriate for the 2D density and $C_{2\mathrm{D}}$ is a geometry-dependent constant (e.g., $C_{2\mathrm{D}}\approx3.9852$ for a square cell and $\approx3.9590$ for a hexagonal cell). The corresponding leading asymptotic kinetic-energy finite-size correction follows from a small-$k$ Jastrow $u(k)\sim -a/k^{3/2}$ and is
      $$
      \Delta T=\frac{C_{2\mathrm{D}} N a}{4\pi P^{5/4}}+O(N^{-1/2}),
      $$
      implying that the energy-per-particle finite-size error in 2D behaves as $O(N^{-5/4})$ asymptotically; for a 2D HEG in the RPA limit $a=\sqrt{2}\pi r_s$.

Let the two-layer effective mass formula be M = μ4 / [ vF2 C1 ] * sqrt( 1 + C1 / μ3 ), with μ = δ1 + δ2 and C1 = 2 γ N2 (ℏ vF)2 where γ = e2 / [ 8π ε0 εd D3 ]. Because γ ∝ D−3 enters multiplicatively into C1, the effective mass M increases as interlayer separation D increases within this formula; this dependence on D arises from the quasi-relativistic (Dirac-like) kinetic structure used to derive the dispersion rather than from a nonrelativistic Schrödinger description, in which the exciton mass does not acquire such a D-dependence.
